chunk_summary: Tube weaning after acquired brain injury requires meeting criteria for hydration, weight gain, and respiratory stability, with staged weaning and instrumental testing to guide safe transitions.
learning_objectives: Understand criteria for tube weaning in children post-acquired brain injury.
Learn the role of instrumental testing (VFSS/FEES) in feeding management decisions.
Recognize risks of premature tube removal and strategies for staged weaning.
Apply neurorecovery principles to feeding support plans.
key_facts: Tube weaning criteria include stable respiratory status, adequate oral hydration without aspiration, and weight gain over several weeks.
Instrumental testing (VFSS or FEES) is recommended when thin-liquid reintroduction or ongoing tube use is questioned.
Premature tube removal can lead to dehydration, aspiration pneumonia, and feeding aversion.
Staged weaning involves maintaining safe oral pleasure feeds while the tube ensures hydration.
Reassessment should only occur when results will change management decisions.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Instrumental testing (VFSS or FEES) is most useful when thin-liquid reintroduction is plausible or when ongoing tube use is being questioned; the output must specify the minimum effective restrictions and compensations that work.
document_key_topics: Tube weaning criteria after acquired brain injury
Staged weaning and neurorecovery feeding strategies
